1: 'Ārabhttā lĕrê'eḵā = 'you have pledged surety for your neighbor' — 'ārab (surety/pledge) is the financial-legal term for cosigning someone else's debt. The father warns not against helping a neighbor but against the specific legal exposure of pledging surety — a common path to financial ruin in the ancient world.
2: 'Snared (nôqaštā) by the words of your mouth' — the trap is self-made; the words you spoke legally bind you. The same speech that creates relationship (promises, pledges) can destroy it through careless commitment.
3: 'Do this now, my son, and deliver yourself' — the urgency is notable: when you realize you are snared, act immediately. Hitra'pēś = 'be earnest/importune' in seeking release — literally 'stamp yourself' or 'abase yourself.' The pride that must be swallowed to seek release is part of the wisdom lesson.
4: 'Give no sleep to your eyes, no slumber to your eyelids' (al-titten šēnāh lĕ'êneyḵā) — the urgency of escaping the pledge is contrasted implicitly with the sluggard's love of sleep (vv9-10). When a real problem demands immediate action, sleep is the enemy.
5: 'Save yourself like a gazelle from the hunter's hand' — the animal-flight imagery emphasizes urgency and self-preservation. The gazelle (ṣĕbî) and the bird (ṣippôr) are creatures that survive by swiftness of escape; the wisdom student must imitate their speed when trapped.
6: 'Go to the ant, you sluggard' (lēk-'el-nĕmālāh 'āṣēl) — nĕmālāh (ant) appears only twice in the OT (here and 30:25). The ant is the unexpected teacher: a creature with no king or overseer that nevertheless stores food for winter. The sluggard ('āṣēl) is the foil throughout vv6-11.
7: The ant works without authority (qāṣîn = officer, šōṭēr = overseer, mōšēl = ruler) compelling her. The self-motivated industry of the ant is the implicit contrast with the sluggard who requires external pressure. Wisdom is self-directing; folly is reactive and passive.
8: 'She prepares her bread in summer and gathers her food at harvest' (tākîn baqqayiṣ laḥmāh) — the ant's wisdom is temporal: she works in the season of abundance for the season of scarcity. The harvesting vocabulary (qāṣîr) links the ant to the broader wisdom theme of providential preparation.
9: The rhetorical questions directed at the sluggard — 'how long will you lie, O sluggard?' (ad-mātay 'āṣēl tiŝkāb) — are not answered; the sluggard's response in v10 is only more sleep. The question is not a real inquiry but a rebuke disguised as curiosity.
10: 'A little sleep, a little slumber, a little folding of the hands to rest' (me'aṭ šēnôt me'aṭ tenûmôt) — three minimizing phrases in a row, each reducing the rest to 'a little.' The self-deception is the point: each 'just a little more' accumulates to a life of failure.
11: 'Poverty will come upon you like a vagabond/traveler' (ûbā' kemĕhallēk rē'šeḵā) — the image of poverty arriving like an unexpected traveler stresses its certainty and suddenness. Māgēn (shield/armed man) — the MKT 'armed man' captures the aggressive, irresistible nature of the approaching want.
12: 'Îš bĕliyya'al = 'man of worthlessness/belial' — bĕliyya'al is a compound term (beli + ya'al = 'without profit/worth') describing someone of absolute moral worthlessness. Hôlēk 'iqqĕšût peh = 'walking with a crooked mouth' — the troublemaker's crooked speech precedes him in his path.
13: The troublemaker communicates deceptively through body language: winking eyes (qōrēṣ bĕ'ênāw), shuffling feet (mōlēl bĕraglāw), pointing fingers (mōreh bĕ'eṣbe'ōtāw). All three are non-verbal signals used to communicate malice while maintaining verbal deniability.
14: 'Perversity is in his heart' (tahpuḵôt bĕlibbô) — tahpuḵôt (perversities/distortions) denotes a fundamental inversion of values. Ḥōrēš rā' = 'devising/plowing evil'; yĕšallēaḥ mĕdānîm = 'sending out discord/strife' — the troublemaker is an active agent of communal damage.
15: 'Therefore his calamity will come suddenly' ('al-kēn pittō'om yābô' 'êdô) — the sudden destruction of the troublemaker mirrors the sudden arrival of poverty for the sluggard (v11). Peta' (suddenly) is emphatic; wĕ'ên marpē' = 'and there is no healing/remedy' — this ruin is irreversible.
16: The numerical saying ('six things... seven') is a wisdom genre (cf. Amos 1-2; Prov 30:15,18,21,29) in which the 'n+1' structure creates suspense and emphasizes the final item. The list that follows (vv17-19) catalogs the behaviors YHWH finds detestable (tô'ēbâ).
17: 'Haughty eyes' ('ênayim rāmôt, lit. 'lifted eyes') head the list. The seven abominations: (1) haughty eyes, (2) lying tongue (lĕšôn šāqer), (3) hands that shed innocent blood (yādayim šōpĕkôt dām nāqî). The list moves from internal attitude (pride) to speech to action, showing the descent of wickedness from disposition to deed.
18: Continuing the list: (4) a heart that devises wicked plans (lēb ḥōrēš maḥšĕbôt 'āwen), (5) feet that run quickly to evil (raglayim mĕmahărôt lārûṣ lārā'āh). The heart, feet, and earlier the tongue and hands create a whole-body portrait of wickedness — no faculty is excluded from the service of evil.
19: Items (6) and (7): a false witness who breathes out lies (mēpîaḥ kĕzābîm 'ēd šāqer) and one who sows discord among brothers (ûmĕšallēaḥ mĕdānîm bên 'aḥîm). Item 7 — discord-sowing — is the culminating abomination, returning to the troublemaker portrait of vv12-14. Communal harmony is the supreme social good whose violation is most detested.
20: 'Keep the commandment of your father' (nĕṣōr miṣwat 'ābîḵā) — the family-wisdom frame resumes. Miṣwāh (commandment) and tôrāh (instruction/teaching) placed in parallel reflect the convergence of family wisdom and covenant instruction in Proverbs: the father's teaching is functionally Torah.
21: 'Bind them on your heart continually' (qāšrēm 'al-libbẹḵā tāmîd) and 'tie them around your neck' ('anddēm 'al-gargrōteḵā) — the physical-binding metaphor parallels the tefillin/mezuzah commands in Deut 6:8-9, suggesting that wisdom instruction has the same binding authority as covenant law. The MKT 'bind them on your heart' preserves this.
22: 'When you walk, it will lead you' (bĕhithalleḵḵā tanhēh 'ōtāḵ); 'when you lie down, it will watch over you' (bĕšāḵbĕkā tiŝmōr 'ālêḵā); 'when you wake, it will talk with you' (wăhăqiṣōtā hî' tĕśîḥeḵā) — the wisdom that is internalized (vv20-21) becomes a constant guide and companion throughout every state of daily life.
23: 'For the command is a lamp and the teaching is a light' (kî nēr miṣwāh wĕtôrāh 'ôr) — nēr (lamp) and 'ôr (light) are distinct: the command is a handheld lamp (immediate, portable guidance); the teaching is light itself (the broader illumination of understanding). Together they define 'the way of life' (derek ḥayyîm). Psalm 119:105 applies the same imagery to the word of God.
24: 'To guard you from the evil woman' (lĕšomrĕkā mê'ēšet rā') — the lamp/light of wisdom has a specific protective function: to guard against the evil woman/adulteress (nēkrîyyāh, foreign woman). Ḥĕlaqat lāšôn nākrîyyāh = 'the smooth tongue of the foreign woman' — the honeyed speech of ch5:3 returns.
25: 'Do not desire her beauty in your heart' (al-taḥmod yopyāh bĕlibbẹkā) — the inner prohibition precedes the outer: desire is the first temptation. The eyelid ('appappayim, unique term) reference describes seductive glancing. The command parallels the 10th commandment's prohibition on covetousness, grounding the adultery warning in covenant ethics.
26: The economic comparison: a prostitute's price is a loaf of bread, but the adulterous wife hunts for a precious life (nāpĕš yĕqārāh tāṣûd). The contrast is stark: the prostitute's transaction costs money; the adultery with a married woman costs everything — the 'precious life' is the adulterer's own.
27: 'Can a man carry fire in his bosom (ḥēqô) and his clothes not be burned?' — the rhetorical question makes the adultery-is-fire analogy obvious. Ḥēq (bosom/lap) is deliberately physical; the fire-in-the-bosom image describes an intimate destruction that cannot be contained.
28: 'Or can one walk on hot coals and his feet not be burned?' — the second rhetorical question extends the fire imagery. The point is not that fire is powerful but that fire is fire: its destructive effect is natural and inevitable, not a matter of luck or circumstance.
29: 'So is the man who goes in to his neighbor's wife' (kēn habbā' el-'ēšet rē'ēhû) — the explicit application of the two fire-rhetorical-questions. The adultery is not merely immoral but physically self-destructive; the 'none who touches her will go unpunished' (lō' yinnāqeh) is the same language used for YHWH 'not clearing the guilty' in Exodus 34:7.
30: 'People do not despise a thief if he steals to satisfy his hunger' — the comparison is surprising: the thief who steals for hunger has some degree of understandable motive, even though he will still pay the penalty. This introduces a comparison to highlight how much worse adultery is.
31: 'But if he is caught, he will pay sevenfold' (yĕšallēm šib'ātayim) — the sevenfold penalty exceeds Exodus 22:4's double-restitution requirement, suggesting that Proverbs is using a proverbial figure for 'complete/total restitution' rather than a specific legal quantity. The thief, though his motive is understandable, still bears full consequences.
32: 'He who commits adultery lacks sense' (nō'ēp 'iššāh ḥasar-lēb) — ḥasar-lēb (lacking heart/mind) is Proverbs' clinical diagnosis: not wickedness primarily but stupidity. The adulterer destroys his own life (mĕšaḥēt napšô hû' = 'he who destroys himself does it') — the act is self-harm in the most fundamental sense.
33: 'Wounds and dishonor he will get' (nega' wĕqālôn yimṣā') — the physical violence implied (nega' = wound/blow) anticipates the cuckolded husband's vengeance in v34. Wĕḥerpātô lō' timmāḥeh = 'and his reproach will not be wiped away' — unlike theft, the shame of adultery is permanent; there is no restitution that restores reputation.
34: 'For jealousy makes a man furious' (kî qin'āh ḥĕmat-gāber) — the husband's jealousy (qin'āh) is presented as natural and fierce: a force that cannot be reasoned with. Lō' yaḥmôl bĕyôm nāqām = 'he will not spare in the day of vengeance' — the day of reckoning is certain.
35: 'He will not accept any ransom, nor be appeased though you multiply gifts' — in contrast to the thief who can make full restitution (v31), there is no financial resolution for adultery's offense against a marriage. No kōper (ransom/compensation) satisfies. The honor offense is categorical and irredeemable — the ultimate demonstration of why adultery, unlike theft, destroys the adulterer completely.
